Joseph let Judah speak. He found his brothers humbled for their sins, remembering him, because Judah had mentioned him twice in his speech, respectful to their father, and very gentle toward their brother Benjamin. They were now ready for the comfort he intended by revealing himself. Joseph ordered all of his attendants to leave. Christ makes himself and his loving-kindness known to his people, out of the sight and hearing of the world. Joseph shed tears of tenderness and strong affection, and with these cast off the austerity with which he had previously behaved toward his brothers. This represents God’s compassion toward returning penitents. “I am Joseph, your brother.” This would humble them even more for their sin in selling him, but it would also encourage them to hope for kind treatment. Thus, when Christ wanted to convince Paul, he said, I am Jesus; and when he wanted to comfort his disciples, he said, It is I, do not be afraid. When Christ manifests himself to his people, he encourages them to draw near to him with a true heart. Joseph does so and shows them that whatever they had planned against him, God had brought good out of it. Sinners must grieve and be angry with themselves for their sins, even though God brings good out of them, because that is no thanks to them. The agreement between all this and the case of a sinner, on Christ’s manifesting himself to his soul, is very striking. He does not, because of this, think sin is less, but a greater evil; yet he is so armed against despair as even to rejoice in what God has wrought, while he trembles thinking of the dangers and destruction from which he has escaped. Joseph promised to take care of his father and all the family. It is the duty of children, if their parents’ needs require it at any time, to support and supply them to the utmost of their ability; this is showing piety at home. After Joseph embraced Benjamin, he caressed them all, and then his brothers talked freely with him about all the affairs of their father's house. After the tokens of true reconciliation with the Lord Jesus, sweet communion with him follows.

Pharaoh was kind to Joseph, and to his relatives for his sake. Egypt would make up for their losses from being removed. Thus those for whom Christ intends his heavenly glory should not regard the things of this world. The best of its enjoyments are but temporary possessions; we cannot be sure of them while here, much less can we carry them away with us. Let us not set our eyes or hearts upon the world; there are better things for us in that blessed land, where Christ, our Joseph, has gone to prepare a place. Joseph dismissed his brothers with a timely caution, “See that you fall not out by the way.” He knew they were too quick to quarrel; and having forgiven them all, he lays this charge upon them, not to reproach one another. Our Lord Jesus has given us this command, that we love one another, and that whatever happens or has happened, we fall not out. For we are brothers; we have all one Father. We are all guilty, and instead of quarrelling with one another, have reason to fall out with ourselves. We are, or hope to be, forgiven by God, whom we have all offended, and therefore should be ready to forgive one another. We are “by the way,” a way through the land of Egypt, where many eyes are upon us, seeking advantage against us; a way that leads to the heavenly Canaan, where we hope to be forever in perfect peace.

To hear that Joseph is alive is too good news to be true; Jacob faints, because he does not believe it. We faint because we do not believe. At length, Jacob is convinced of the truth. Jacob was old and did not expect to live long. He says, Let my eyes be refreshed with this sight before they are closed, and then I need no more to make me happy in this world. Behold Jesus manifesting himself as a Brother and Friend to those who once were his despisers, his enemies. He assures them of his love and the riches of his grace. He commands them to lay aside envy, anger, malice, and strife, and to live in peace with each other. He teaches them to give up the world for him and his fullness. He supplies all that is needful to bring them home to himself, so that where he is they may be also. And though when he at last sends for his people, they may for a time feel some doubts and fears, yet the thought of seeing his glory and of being with him will enable them to say, It is enough; I am willing to die; and I go to see and to be with the Beloved of my soul.
